title: Fourier Series: Building Waves from Sines

steps.title: The basic move
steps.body: A Fourier series tries to build a periodic function by adding sine and cosine waves. The first few terms give a rough silhouette; later terms add sharper detail.

steps.title: Coefficient intuition
steps.prompt: If the Fourier coefficients shrink quickly, what should happen to the partial sums?
choices: They must oscillate forever with no improvement.
explanations: Correct. Smaller high-frequency coefficients usually mean the later corrections are gentler.
Not quite. Oscillation can remain, but smaller coefficients usually make the approximation improve.
No. A finite sum of periodic sine and cosine waves is still periodic.

steps.title: Watch the partial sums
steps.prompt: Increase the number of terms. The square wave gets sharper, but pay attention to what happens near the jump.

steps.title: Near the jump
steps.prompt: For the square wave, what does adding more terms do near a discontinuity?
choices: It removes every trace of overshoot immediately.
It narrows the overshoot region, but a visible overshoot remains.
explanations: The approximation improves away from the jump, but the overshoot is stubborn.
Correct. This is the visual footprint of the Gibbs phenomenon.
The period is set by the frequencies in the series, not by the number of terms shown here.
